---
title: "Nvidia just won't work! Why, why, why?!"
date: 2008-08-08
forum: Multimedia Software
---

### Post by Aktrop on 2008-08-08
I used to run dual monitors (one DVI, one VGA) and after much tweaking, both worked with this xorg.conf file:

```

# nvidia-settings: X configuration file generated by nvidia-settings
# nvidia-settings:  version 1.0  (buildd@vernadsky)  Tue Mar  4 20:24:34 UTC 2008

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Layout0"
    Screen      0  "Screen0" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Keyboard0" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Mouse0" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Files"
    RgbPath         "/usr/X11R6/lib/X11/rgb"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "dbe"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
    Option         "Xinerama" "0"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Mouse0"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "no"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Keyboard0"
    Driver         "kbd"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    # HorizSync source: xconfig, VertRefresh source: xconfig
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "CRT-0"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 83.0
    VertRefresh     56.0 - 76.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Videocard0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 7300 GS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "TwinView" "1"
    Option         "TwinViewXineramaInfoOrder" "DFP-1"
    Option         "metamodes" "CRT: 1280x1024 +1280+0, DFP: nvidia-auto-select +0+0"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```

I recently upgraded to from my VGA screen to a DVI screen, which is identical to the other DVI screen I was using.

But now I just can't get it work. My xorg.conf is completely broken. When I reboot in recovery mode, and try to run sudo nvidia-config, that xorg doesn't work either. And when I reboot in recovery mode, sudo nvidia-settings won't work either because it's not using the nvidia driver!

I tried reinstalling the nvidia drivers - using the latest ones - via the procedure outlined here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=797270](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=797270) but no luck there either.

I'm not sure this will help but here's the output of 

```
lspci -n | grep 0300
``` is

```

01:00.0 0300: 10de:01df (rev a1)


```

and the output of ```
lspci | grep VGA
``` is

```

01:00.0 VGA compatible controller: nVidia Corporation G71 [GeForce 7300 GS] (rev a1)



```

I'm at my wit's end. Please help.

---

### Post by SuperSonic4 on 2008-08-08
Have you tried using nvidia's GUI

```
gksudo nvidia-settings
``` and looking at it from there

---

### Post by stchman on 2008-08-08
If you are running the restricted driver I recommend installing the Nvidia control panel.

```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-settings
```

After that is installed you will need to run the app as sudo.

There will be a section that will let you configure multiple monitors.

---

### Post by Aktrop on 2008-08-08
Thanks for the quick replies. And yes, I have tried that. The problem is that when I use sudo nvidia-settings, it tells me that I'm not currently using the nvidia driver. 

If I use nvidia-config and restart the X server, then both my screens go into power saving mode and I see nothing. So the only way for me to see anything on the screen with gdm running is in recovery mode, which doesn't use the nvidia driver. 

Hence my quandry :confused:

### Post by drawhole on 2008-08-08
hey i'm not super experanced but i've had some odd nvidia stuff happen to ... i'm running hardy ..... anyway this may or may not work but try removing everything to do with nivida and starting over, i've found somethings this will solve the issuse.. i'm not useing 2 monitors but i have have my screen go into standby and have to use the recovey..... 


what i did when that happened was 2 things first cheaked the xorg but you've done that... so i would suggest that you remove all the nvida componits ... and make sure you getting the right one (eg nvidia-glx-new or nvidia-glx.. ) as for the settings, i've never used them... on my friend comp that has 2 monitors i set it up the same way i wounld on my system and his worked great ...... sry i can't be of more help but it's something to try ..


o ya something else i just remembered on my friends dell ... for what ever reason it picked the wrong pci addy you can try playing with it to.   

BusID          "PCI:1:0:0" for my card this is 0:5:0

thats all i can offer thou.

### Post by Anlace on 2008-08-08
I too struggled with resolution issues for the past year or more (no kidding).  After much research and a lot of work I found that the answer was that my monitor was passing corrupted EDID info to xorg.  I just posted my solution to this, take a look at [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=884211](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=884211) and see if this helps.

---

### Post by Anlace on 2008-08-09
Alright, glad I can help :-)  You've got a good start and it makes things easier now that you know you've got a corrupt EDID to deal with.

Start by powering off the computer, unplug the faulty monitor and boot back up into the correct resolution.

Instead you could use the VGA (analog) connection if that gives you the correct resolution.  That is what I did to get a working edid.bin.

Do you have the Nvidia driver installed?  If you do then you'll also have nvidia-settings.  In Kubuntu just type nvidia-settings into "Run Command..." from the KDE menu or type nvidia-settings in a console.

I'm not sure where you'll find nvidia-settings in Ubuntu but I'm willing to bet it's not hidden if you go through your program menu.  It's called Nvidia X Server Settings and has a Nvidia logo icon, in Kubuntu it's in the KDE menu under Settings.

Once Nvidia X Server Settings is open identify the monitor that is sending correct EDID information to xorg.  It's likely to be either DFP-0 or DFP-1 if you've got two flat panels (LCDs) or CRT-0 etc for, well, CRTs.  (I have read that a VGA (analog) connection will sometime identify as CRT even if it's a flat panel.)

You can find the monitor listed under the GPU section in Nvidia Settings, look for the arrow next to it - if the arrow is pointing at GPU then click it to open the sub-menu underneath, there in the list of setting options you'll find your monitor as DFP-0 or CRT-1 or whatever it is.  If you have only one monitor connected at boot up there will be only one monitor listed.

Now click on the monitor name for its settings (DFP-0 in my case) and you'll see info for it in the right side panel.  At the bottom of that panel, just above the Quit button, is a button for Acquire EDID...  Once you press that button the title of the window that opens will say "Please select file where EDID data will be saved."  I believe it will default to your home directory.  I created a folder called Nvidia and saved the file edid.bin inside of it.

Now open xorg.conf to edit it.  Type sudo kate /etc/X11/xorg.conf for KDE or sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf for Gnome.  Under the Device section add the line:
Option "CustomEDID" "DFP-0:/path/to/file/edid.bin"

Make sure to change the path to the exact location where you saved your edid.bin file, also make sure that you have the correct identifier for your monitor as well (mine is DFP-0).

If I were you I would use this for both monitors, adding a line for each one like this:

Option "CustomEDID" "DFP-0:/path/to/file/edid.bin"
Option "CustomEDID" "DFP-1:/path/to/file/edid.bin"

Here is exactly what my working xorg.conf file looks like.  As you can see the only change I have made from the basic xorg.conf created at system install was to change the driver to "nvidia" and to add the CustomEDID line.

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Configured Video Device"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	Option		"CustomEDID"	"DFP-0:/home/gail/Nvidia/edid.bin"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
EndSection

After over a year frustrated that nothing would let me use Nvidia drivers and native resolution I finally have a working setup and couldn't be happier.  I hope this works as well for you too.

Peace,
Gail

---

### Post by Aktrop on 2008-08-09

I've managed to get native resolution by using a VGA cable. I fired up nvidia-settings, but I can't find the Acquire EDID button. I've attached a screenshot of the relevant window. Nothing happens when I click on the monitor name. Is there something simple I'm missing?

### Post by Anlace on 2008-08-09
Since both your monitors are identical, I would try connecting the other one to get a valid edid.

If there is still no edid info reported by the Nvidia driver then try booting up with FailSafe and using XFix to reset your xorg.conf.  It may be that you've got a line or two (or three) in xorg.conf that is not allowing for edid to be picked up by the Nvidia driver.

Here's a screen shot of what Nvidia X Server Settings looks like for me.

### Post by Aktrop on 2008-08-09
Hi Gail - ok, so let me sum up the adventure since I last posted.

(1) After some fiddling, I managed to get the Acquire EDID button. This button showed up when I plugged both screens in (one DVI, the other VGA). Oddly enough, it correctly identified the screen on the nvidia-settings page as a Lenovo 193p (oddly because the VGA monitor usually shows up as "Generic"). When I prompted nvidia-settings to detect the other screen it failed to do so. I followed your instructions, changing two things in the xorg file: (a) the driver (2) customEDID. But this didn't actually do the trick.

(2) Having made those changes, I thought I could just plug in my old xorg.conf file, with key values suitably changed, to run both monitors, but no luck at all. I'm scaling back to just try and get ONE monitor working correctly.

(3) I'm not sure that EDID is the core issue. I'm currently working on one VGA cabled monitor with this xorg.conf file:

    VertRefresh     56.0 - 76.0
    Gamma           1
    ModeLine       "640x480@60" 25.2 640 656 752 800 480 490 492 525 -hsync -vsync
    ModeLine       "800x600@56" 36.0 800 824 896 1024 600 601 603 625 +hsync +vsync
    ModeLine       "800x600@60" 40.0 800 840 968 1056 600 601 605 628 +hsync +vsync
    ModeLine       "1024x768@60" 65.0 1024 1048 1184 1344 768 771 777 806 -hsync -vsync
    ModeLine       "1280x960@60" 102.1 1280 1360 1496 1712 960 961 964 994 -hsync +vsync
    ModeLine       "1280x1024@60" 108.0 1280 1328 1440 1688 1024 1025 1028 1066 +hsync +vsync
EndSection

Section "Device"

#Option		"IgnoreEDID" "1"
	#Option 	"UseDisplayDevice" "DFP-0"
	#Option	"UseCustomEDID" "DFP-0:/home/kranti/edid.bin"
    Identifier     "Videocard0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 7300 GS"
EndSection

Section "Device"

	#Option		"IgnoreEDID" "1"
    Identifier     "Videocard1"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 7300 GS"
    Option         "UseDisplayDevice" "DFP-0"
    Option         "UseCustomEDID" "DFP-0:/home/kranti/edid.bin"
    BusID          "PCI:1:0:0"
    Screen          1
EndSection

Section "Screen"

 # 
    Identifier     "screen1"
    Device         "Videocard1"
    Monitor        "Monitor1"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "TwinView" "0"
    Option         "metamodes" "DFP-0: 1280x1024 +0+0"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
    EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Screen"

# Removed Option "metamodes" "CRT: 1280x1024@60 +0+0; CRT: 800x600@56 +0+0; CRT: 1280x960@60 +0+0; CRT: 640x480@60 +0+0"
# Removed Option "TwinView" "0"
# Removed Option "metamodes" "1280x1024 +0+0"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "TwinView" "1"
    Option         "TwinViewXineramaInfoOrder" "CRT-0"
    Option         "metamodes" "CRT: 1280x1024 +1280+0, DFP: nvidia-auto-select +0+0"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
    EndSubSection
EndSection


```

Sorry for all the verbosity... but I hope it helps *someone*!

---

### Post by Anlace on 2008-08-09
Nice to see the progress :-)

TwinView/Xinerama does complicate things a bit.

A couple of thoughts, I'm not sure if this will be totally helpful but might be worth fiddling with.

First I'm not sure that you need all the metamode and modeline calls.  The EDID info you are handing to the Nvidia driver is supplying all of that information.  You might want to try commenting all of them out.

Another thought is that when you switch cables you'll need to be sure that all the CRT references are changed to DFP-1.

There's a lot going on in this xorg.conf file that I don't believe needs to be there but I'm too tired to dive into it at the moment.  Be patient and I'll compare yours with mine and read Nvidia's suggestions for xinerama.

In the meantime, what resolution is native for the monitors?  Are you using two separate video cards, both of them GeForce 7300 GS?  Or is there only one with two DVI ports?

I'm happy to help, it's good to be able to give back to the community in some way.

Peace,
Gail

### Post by Anlace on 2008-08-10
Ok, I am back and more awake this time (and caffeinated).

First there's very good stuff in Nvidia's Help file.  This is a link to the one for the latest 64-bit driver (173.14.12):
[http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86_64/173.14.12/README/index.html](http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86_64/173.14.12/README/index.html)

And for the 32-bit driver (173.14.12):
[http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/173.14.12/README/index.html](http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/173.14.12/README/index.html)

As far as I can see they are the same.  It's a royal crap-load of information but it does contain some excellent troubleshooting info.  Just put your hip waders on and be prepared to learn something.

Here is specific info for setting up dual monitors:

Chapter 13. Configuring TwinView
[http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/173.14.12/README/chapter-13.html](http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/173.14.12/README/chapter-13.html)

And this chapter lists ALL of the xorg.conf edits and what they do:
Appendix B. X Config Options
[http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/173.14.12/README/appendix-b.html](http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/173.14.12/README/appendix-b.html)

You might want to try this xorg.conf and see if it works (backup the current xorg.conf first).  As you can see it is a lot simpler than what you had going:

```
Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Videocard"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 7300 GS"
    BusID          "PCI:1:0:0"
    Option         "UseCustomEDID" "CRT-0:/home/kranti/edid.bin"
    Option         "UseCustomEDID" "DFP-0:/home/kranti/edid.bin"
    Option         "TwinView"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"CRT-0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"DFP-0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Screen1"
	Monitor		"CRT-0"
	Device		"Videocard"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Screen2"
	Monitor		"DFP-0"
	Device		"Videocard"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
EndSection
```

In this xorg.conf file I assumed that you are using a single video card and two identical monitors.  The Nvidia driver will automatically determine which monitor to display on which side (left or right).  If that is messed up then go back to the Nvidia help file (link above) and see what they suggest for fixing that.

I also assumed that you are using a typical GeForce 7300 GS card which has one DVI (DFP) connector and one VGA (CRT) connector.  Nvidia's driver is always going to assume a CRT on the VGA connector even if it's an LCD monitor.  

The idea is to start simple, and then add the elements that make sense to address specific problems.  In the xorg.conf above by using Option UseCustomEdid we have given very specific display directions to the Nvidia driver with edid.bin so you don't need to give it metamodes, modelines, refresh rates, or anything like that.  It's all contained in edid.bin.

Always hand-edit your xorg.conf (backing up the original of course) to include the various options suggested in Nvidia's help file.  Nvidia's utility nvidia-xconfig only seems to work effectively if your monitor(s) have good edid info, I suspect the same is also true for Gnome or KDE monitor/display settings utilities too.

Let me know how this goes for you.  I'm happy to help.

Peace,
Gail

### Post by Aktrop on 2008-08-10
Ok, some progress to report: working off your sample xorg.conf I've come up (with some help from "nvidia-xconfig --twinview") an xorg that is semi-stable. Caveats:
(1) It gives me both screens BUT only after I cold start AND at first only one screen comes to life, and the other does after I fire up nvidia-settings (without actually messing with any of the settings there). 
(2) The CRT screen is a little bit jittery/flickery.

I've attached a screenshot of nvidia-settings.

Here's the xorg:

```

# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder57)  Thu Jul 17 18:39:19 PDT 2008

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Screen0" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "glx"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "CRT"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 81.0
    VertRefresh     56.0 - 76.0
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "DFP"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 81.0
    VertRefresh     56.0 - 76.0
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Videocard"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
    BoardName      "GeForce 7300 GS"
    BusID          "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard"
    Monitor        "CRT"
    Option         "UseCustomEDID" "CRT:/home/kranti/edid.bin"
    Option         "UseDisplayDevice" "CRT, DFP"
    Option         "TwinView" "True"
    Option         "MetaModes" "nvidia-auto-select, nvidia-auto-select"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Modes      "nvidia-auto-select"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Videocard"
    Monitor        "DFP"
EndSection



```

Now if I can solve the jitter/flicker issue and reliability, I'll be happy. Thank you so much for all the help -- and patience!!

---

### Post by Aktrop on 2008-08-12
I finally got it to work. I had to go and buy myself another VGA-DVI adapter, since nothing I did could bring the DFP to life. 

I came *this* close to buying XP. Setting up dual monitors shouldn't take four days. 

For a unsophisticated user like myself - and perhaps you! - I learnt to use the xorg.conf log file, which is full of gobbledygook but is also pretty intelligible at crucial points. It's typically at /var/log/xorg.0.log. Have look at it if you're having trouble. Mine told me that EDID values weren't being used, and even when I acquired EDID values and used the option to use those custom EDIDs, X STILL didn't use them. I have no idea why. But I would never have figured it out without reading the log. 

And I would never have figured it out without the community's inputs. So thanks people - you're the best and you always redeem my faith in open source software.

(p.s.: My significant other uses XP. I keep urging her to switch. She smiles and says "I would love to, but I just don't have the TIME!" (After seeing me going hammer and tongs for 4-5days trying to get this to work!)

---

### Post by hooby3dfx on 2008-10-02
I have a similar really annoying problem.  
I'm using a GeForce 6600GT AGP and a Samsung SyncMaster 710N.
Ive been trying to figure out why I cant get a higher res than 1024x768, when the native res is 1280x1024, and I know it works because it does in Windows!! (Dual booting)
The monitor is plugged into my VGA port.  
My output of sudo get-edid | parse-edid:```

parse-edid: parse-edid version 1.4.1
get-edid: get-edid version 1.4.1

	Performing real mode VBE call
	Interrupt 0x10 ax=0x4f00 bx=0x0 cx=0x0
	Function supported
	Call successful

	VBE version 300
	VBE string at 0x11110 "NVIDIA"

VBE/DDC service about to be called
	Report DDC capabilities

	Performing real mode VBE call
	Interrupt 0x10 ax=0x4f15 bx=0x0 cx=0x0
	Function supported
	Call successful

	Monitor and video card combination does not support DDC1 transfers
	Monitor and video card combination supports DDC2 transfers
	0 seconds per 128 byte EDID block transfer
	Screen is not blanked during DDC transfer

Reading next EDID block

VBE/DDC service about to be called
	Read EDID

	Performing real mode VBE call
	Interrupt 0x10 ax=0x4f15 bx=0x1 cx=0x0
	Function supported
	Call successful

parse-edid: EDID checksum passed.

	# EDID version 1 revision 1
Section "Monitor"
	# Block type: 2:0 3:fd
	Identifier "SAM:5660"
	VendorName "SAM"
	ModelName "SAM:5660"
	# Block type: 2:0 3:fd
	HorizSync 30-61
	VertRefresh 50-75
	# Max dot clock (video bandwidth) 80 MHz
	# DPMS capabilities: Active off:yes  Suspend:yes  Standby:yes

	Mode 	"640x480"	# vfreq 75.000Hz, hfreq 37.500kHz
		DotClock	31.500000
		HTimings	640 656 720 840
		VTimings	480 481 484 500
		Flags	"-HSync" "-VSync"
	EndMode
	Mode 	"800x600"	# vfreq 75.000Hz, hfreq 46.875kHz
		DotClock	49.500000
		HTimings	800 816 896 1056
		VTimings	600 601 604 625
		Flags	"+HSync" "+VSync"
	EndMode
	Mode 	"1024x768"	# vfreq 75.029Hz, hfreq 60.023kHz
		DotClock	78.750000
		HTimings	1024 1040 1136 1312
		VTimings	768 769 772 800
		Flags	"+HSync" "+VSync"
	EndMode
	# Block type: 2:0 3:fd
EndSection

```
So...
Either my Linux install cant get the correct EDID, or my video card is outputting a slightly wrong EDID but Windows doesnt mind.
Any ideas??
Bug?

EDIT:
It turned out that my monitor was actually putting out incorrect data in the EDID, but I was able to make a backup from EDID information stored in the Windows registry, copy that file into my Linux partition, and use CustomEDID.
SUCCESS!
Thanks!!

---

### Post by al108 on 2008-10-20
Well, I have LG L246W monitor, which has HDMI input instead of DVI. My problem was that videocard (8800GT G92, and now GTX260) reads it as a HDTV monitor instead of computer monitor and the whole screen was offset about an inch to the left... Same thing in Win, but they have figured out how to override EDID flags in registry. Aquiring EDID with VGA cable worked in Ubuntu though as a quick and dirty solution. Thanks everybody.

---
